text: The surgeon begins pelvic dissection similar to an anterior resection, identifying and dividing the inferior mesenteric pedicle. Using a medial approach, the inferior mesenteric artery is identified by pulling the mesocolon, with the view from the right side. The bifurcation of the artery is visible as dissection proceeds.

The surgeon applies a liga clip and uses a ligasure to divide distally, raising a flap while preserving retroperitoneal structures, including the ureter. The flap is raised away from the Gerota's fascia, following the embryonic vascular plane to minimize bleeding. The surgeon then incises the line of Toldt laterally, which is an avascular attachment along the lateral abdominal wall.

The surgeon dissects retrocholically to reach the splenic flexure of the colon, using the same camera setup. The gastrocolic omentum is divided, and the splenic flexure is fully mobilized. The inferior border of the pancreas is visible, separated from the transverse colon and mesocolon. A small vessel, initially mistaken for the middle colic artery, is clipped and divided near the pancreas.

The surgeon exposes the retroperitoneal structures, highlighting the avascular plane to avoid vessels. The dissection proceeds to the pelvis for total mesorectal excision, preserving vascular and nerve bundles, including sympathetic and parasympathetic nerves. The dissection moves from posterior to anterolateral, reaching the pelvic diaphragm where the levator ani muscle is visible.

The surgeon mobilizes the right colon by incising the peritoneum below the cecum and progressing towards the hepatic flexure. The duodenum is identified, preserved, and pushed back. A retrocolic tunnel is created, allowing access to the vascular pedicle where the middle colic and right colic vessels are skeletonized and divided at their origin.

The surgeon manipulates the hepatic flexure of the colon downward using a cartilaginous maneuver, revealing the duodenum and the head of the pancreas. The middle colic vessels are exposed and approached supracolically, then clipped and divided. This action completes the mobilization of the colon.

The surgeon uses an endocutter to divide the terminal ileum, freeing the specimen at one end. Retracting stitches are applied, and an intrasphincteric dissection is performed from below. Once the internal sphincter is divided, the entire colon, rectum, and a portion of the terminal ileum are delivered out through a transanal approach. Lone star retraction is utilized to aid in this process.

An intracorporeal pouch is created using two firings of a 60mm stapler, resulting in a 12cm pouch. The pouch is then exteriorized and anastomosed to the external sphincter, forming a coloanal anastomosis. A covering ileostomy is performed, and a drain is placed to complete the procedure.
